name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ لَا يَقُومُونَ إِلَّا كَمَا يَقُومُ ٱلَّذِى يَتَخَبَّطُهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ مِنَ ٱلْمَسِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا ٱلْبَيْعُ مِثْلُ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ ۗ وَأَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْبَيْعَ وَحَرَّمَ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ ۚ فَمَن جَآءَهُۥ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ فَٱنتَهَىٰ فَلَهُۥ مَا سَلَفَ وَأَمْرُهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَنْ عَادَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٧٥)
```

```english
Those who exact usury will not stand but like one deranged by the Devil’s touch. That is because they say, ‘Trade is just like usury.’ While Allah has allowed trade and forbidden usury. Whoever relinquishes [usury] on receiving advice from his Lord shall keep [the gains of] what is past, and his matter will rest with Allah. As for those who resume, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (275)
```

```arabic
يَمْحَقُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ وَيُرْبِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ أَثِيمٍ (٢٧٦)
```

```english
Allah brings usury to naught, but He makes charities flourish. Allah does not like any sinful ingrate. (276)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٢٧٧)
```

```english
Indeed those who have faith, do righteous deeds, maintain the prayer and give the zakat, they shall have their reward near their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (277)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَذَرُوا۟ مَا بَقِىَ مِنَ ٱلرِّبَوٰٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٢٧٨)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and abandon [all claims to] what remains of usury, should you be faithful. (278)
```

```arabic
فَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَأْذَنُوا۟ بِحَرْبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۖ وَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَلَكُمْ رُءُوسُ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ لَا تَظْلِمُونَ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ (٢٧٩)
```

```english
And if you do not, then be informed of a war from Allah and His apostle. And if you repent, then you will have your principal, neither harming others, nor suffering harm. (279)
```

```arabic
وَإِن كَانَ ذُو عُسْرَةٍ فَنَظِرَةٌ إِلَىٰ مَيْسَرَةٍ ۚ وَأَن تَصَدَّقُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٨٠)
```

```english
If [the debtor] is in straits, let there be a respite until the time of ease; and if you remit [the debt] as charity, it will be better for you, should you know. (280)
```

```arabic
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ ثُمَّ تُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٢٨١)
```

```english
And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah. Then every soul shall be recompensed fully for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged. (281)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are directed toward emphasizing the prohibition of usury (الربا) and intensifying the warning against usurers; they are not intended as an initial legislation (التشريع الابتدائي). Indeed, their tone is not that of primary legislation. Rather, what is suited for that purpose is the saying of the Exalted in Surah Al-Imran: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَأْكُلُوا الرِّبَا أَضْعَافًا مُّضَاعَفَةً وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** — "O you who have faith! Do not exact usury, twofold and severalfold, and be wary of Allah so that you may be felicitous" (3:130). To be sure, these verses include such statements as His saying: "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and abandon what remains of usury, if you are believers." The context of the verse indicates that the Muslims had not ceased their practice despite the previous prohibition of usury; rather, they continued to circulate it among themselves to some extent. Thus, Allah commanded them to desist from this and to abandon the usury owed by debtors to their creditors (الغرماء). From this, the meaning of His saying becomes clear: "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists, he shall have what is past, and his matter rests with Allah," as will be explained later.

Prior to the prohibition in Surah Al-Imran was the saying of the Exalted in Surah Al-Rum, which is Meccan: **وَمَا آتَيْتُم مِّن رِّبًا لِّيَرْبُوَ فِي أَمْوَالِ النَّاسِ فَلَا يَرْبُو عِندَ اللَّهِ وَمَا آتَيْتُم مِّن زَكَاةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللَّهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ** — "What you give by way of usury, rather it may increase in people’s wealth, does not increase with Allah. But what you give by way of alms, seeking Allah’s pleasure—it is they who will receive a manifold increase" (30:39). From this, it appears that usury was a discouraged matter from the early days of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) before the Migration (الهجرة), until the matter of its prohibition was finalized in Surah Al-Imran. Subsequently, the matter was intensified in Surah Al-Baqarah through these seven verses, whose context indicates that the prohibition had been previously revealed. Thus, it is evident that these verses were revealed after Surah Al-Imran.

Furthermore, the prohibition of usury existed in the religion of the Jews, as mentioned by Allah the Exalted in His saying: **وَأَخْذِهِمُ الرِّبَا وَقَدْ نُهُوا عَنْهُ** — "and for their taking usury though they had been forbidden from it" (4:161). This is also alluded to in His saying—narrating their words—**لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ سَبِيلٌ** — "There is no way against us concerning the unlearned" (3:75). Given the Quran’s confirmation of their Book and the absence of any abrogation (نسخ) of what clearly indicated its prohibition, it remained prohibited in Islam.

The verses—meaning the verses of usury—are not devoid of a connection to the preceding verses regarding spending in the way of Allah, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted within them: "Allah brings usury to naught and gives increase to charities," and His saying: "And that you should give it as charity is better for you." Similarly, its mention in Surah Al-Rum and Surah Al-Imran occurs in conjunction with the mention of spending and charity (الصدقة), and the exhortation and encouragement toward them.

Moreover, reason (الاعتبار) also supports the connection between the two through contrast and opposition. Usury is taking without compensation, just as charity is giving without compensation. The evil effects resulting from usury correspond to and mirror the positive effects resulting from charity in a universal manner, without exception. Every corruption (مفسدة) arising from usury is mirrored by a corresponding benefit (مصلحة) from charity—such as the spreading of mercy and love, the support of the poor and needy, the growth of wealth, the organization of affairs, and the establishment of order and security. The opposite of all this is found in usury.

Allah, the Glorified, has intensified the matter of usury in these verses to a degree not found in any other branch of religion (فروع الدين), except for the befriending of the enemies of the religion; for the intensification therein resembles the intensification regarding usury. As for other major sins (الكبائر), although the Quran has declared opposition to them and used stern language, the tone of the discourse in prohibiting them is less severe than in these two matters—even regarding adultery, wine-drinking, gambling, injustice, and even greater sins like the killing of a soul which Allah has forbidden, and corruption (الفساد). All of these are lesser in severity than usury and the befriending of the enemies of the religion.

This is only because those sins do not extend their ill-omened effects beyond an individual or a few individuals, nor do they spread except to certain aspects of the soul, nor do they govern except specific actions and deeds. This is in contrast to these two sins, for they possess an evil influence that demolishes the foundation of religion and erases its traces, corrupts the system of human life, and casts a veil over the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — causing its judgment to fail until it becomes a thing forgotten and discarded, as will be somewhat clarified, Allah the Almighty willing.

The course of history has confirmed the Book of Allah in its intensification regarding these two matters. Compromise, befriending, affection, and leaning toward the enemies of the religion have brought the Islamic nations down into a pit of destruction where they have become a prey to others—possessing neither wealth, nor honor, nor life. They deserve neither death nor life; they are not permitted to die, nor are they overlooked so they might benefit from the gift of life. Religion has abandoned them, and general virtues have departed from them.

Similarly, the consumption of usury led to the hoarding of treasures, the accumulation of wealth, and dominance (السودد), which in turn dragged the world into global wars and divided humanity into two classes: the prosperous wealthy and the wretched destitute. The gap became clear, and it became an affliction that crushes mountains, shakes the earth, and threatens humanity with collapse and the world with ruin. "Then the fate of those who committed evil was the worst."

It will become clear to you, Allah the Exalted willing, that what Allah the Exalted mentioned regarding usury and the befriending of the enemies of the religion is among the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Noble Quran.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الذين يأكلون الربا لا يقومون إِلا كما يقوم الذي يتخبطه الشيطان من المس** — "Those who devour usury shall not rise except like one whom Satan has confounded by his touch" (2:275). Confounding (الخبط) is to walk without balance or direction. It is said "the camel confounded" (خبط البعير) when its direction of walking becomes disordered. For a human being in life, there is a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — from which he does not deviate; for he inevitably possesses actions and movements in the path of his life according to the environment in which he lives. These actions are maintained in order by rational, conventional judgments (أحكام اعتقادية عقلائية) which man has established and organized, and then applied to his individual and social actions. He intends to eat when hungry, to drink when thirsty, to seek a bed when desiring intimacy, to rest when tired, and to seek shade for dwelling, and so on. He expands toward certain matters and withdraws from others in his social interactions; he desires every means when desiring its end, and when he seeks an effect, he leans toward its cause.

These actions, based on these beliefs, are connected and unified in a harmonious, non-contradictory manner, and their totality constitutes the path of his life.

Man was only guided to this straight path by a power deposited within him, which is the discerning power (القوة المميزة) between good and evil, benefit and harm, and beauty and ugliness. Some discussion on this has already passed.

As for the "touched" person (الممسوس)—the one whose discerning power has become disordered—he does not distinguish between the beautiful and the ugly, the beneficial and the harmful, or good and evil. He applies the judgment of one case to its opposite. This is not because he has forgotten the meaning of beauty or ugliness, for he remains a human being possessed of will (إرادة), and it is impossible for anything other than human actions to proceed from a human. Rather, it is because he sees the ugly as beautiful and the beautiful as ugly, and the good and beneficial as evil and harmful, and vice versa. Thus, he is "confounded" (خابط) in the application of judgments and the identification of cases.

Furthermore, he does not merely treat the unusual as usual without the reverse; for that would imply he possesses organized opinions and thoughts that he occasionally misapplies. Rather, the judgment of the habitual (العادة) and the non-habitual has become disordered for him, and whatever he imagines or desires becomes what he follows. Thus, the habitual and the non-habitual are equal to him, like a camel that strikes and tramples without balance. In his deviation from the habitual, he sees the habitual as nothing more than the deviation itself, with no preference for it. Thus, he is not drawn back from the deviation to the habit. Understand this.

This is the state of the usurer in his taking of usury (giving a thing and taking its equivalent plus an increase due to a delay). That which the primordial nature (الفطرة) calls for and upon which the foundation of human social life is built is that one should trade by exchanging the wealth he has and does not need for the wealth of another that he does need. As for giving wealth and taking its exact equivalent back with an increase, this is something that demolishes the decree of nature and the foundation of livelihood. For this leads, on the part of the usurer, to the misappropriation of wealth from the hand of the debtor and its accumulation and concentration with the usurer; for this wealth continues to grow and increase, and it only grows from the wealth of others—it is by decrease and separation on one side, and increase and joining on the other.

On the part of the debtor who pays the usury, it leads to an increase in expenditure over time—an increase that nothing can compensate for—alongside an increasing need. Whenever the expenditure increases (i.e., the usury grows exponentially), the need increases without anything to repair the deficit or compensate for it. In this lies the destruction of the debtor's life.

Thus, usury contradicts social balance and equilibrium (التعادل الاجتماعي) and corrupts the order governing this human straight path to which the divine nature has guided him.

This is the "confounding" (الخبط) with which the usurer is afflicted, like the confounding of the touched person. For practicing usury forces his fundamental understanding of transaction and exchange to become disordered, such that he does not distinguish between trade (البيع) and usury (الربا). When he is called to abandon usury and take up trade, he replies that trade is just like usury, possessing no advantage over it. Thus, there is no reason to abandon usury and take up trade. For this reason, the Exalted argued for the confounding of the usurers by what He narrated of their saying: "Trade is just like usury."

From this explanation, several points become clear:

**First:** That the meaning of "rising" (القيام) in the saying of the Exalted: "they shall not rise except like one who rises," refers to the stability of life and the management of livelihood. This is one of the meanings of "rising" known to the people of the language in their usage. The Exalted said: **لِيَقُومَ الناسُ بِالْقِسْطِ** — "that mankind may maintain justice" (57:25); and He said: **أَن تَقُومَ السَّمَاءُ وَالْأَرْضُ بِأَمْرِهِ** — "that the sky and the earth stand by His command" (30:25); and He said: **وَأَن تَقُومُوا لِلْيَتَامَىٰ بِالْقِسْطِ** — "and that you maintain justice for the orphans" (4:127). As for it meaning the opposite of sitting, it is not suited to the context, nor would the meaning of the verse be sound based on it.

**Second:** That the "confounding of the touched person" in his rising does not refer to the movements displayed by a touched person during an epileptic fit or following it, as appears from the words of some commentators. For that does not suit the purpose for which the discourse was directed—which is the usurer's belief that there is no difference between trade and usury, and his building his actions upon that. The result is voluntary actions proceeding from a confounded belief. How great is the difference between these and the movements proceeding from an epileptic during a fit! Thus, we must conclude what we mentioned: that it refers to the usurer's "rising" in his life regarding the matter of livelihood, like the rising of the touched person who is confounded in the matter of life.

**Third:** The subtle point (النكتة) in comparing trade to usury rather than the reverse in the saying of the Exalted: "That is because they said: 'Trade is just like usury,'" and He did not say: "Usury is just like trade," as would be the first thought to come to mind. This will be clarified later.

**Fourth:** That the comparison—meaning His saying: "whom Satan has confounded by his touch"—is not devoid of an indication of the possibility of this occurring in cases of madness (الجنون) in general. For although the verse does not prove that every madness is from the touch of Satan, it is not devoid of an indication that some madness is due to the touch of Satan. Similarly, although the verse does not prove that this "touch" is the act of Iblis himself—for "Satan" (الشيطان) means the "evil one" and is applied to Iblis, the evil ones among the jinn, and the evil ones among humans, and Iblis is from the jinn—the certain indication of the verse is that the jinn have a role in some touched persons, if not all of them.

As for what some commentators mentioned—that this comparison is a form of conforming to the common people in some of their false beliefs, as they believed in the jinn's influence over the insane, and that there is no harm in this because it is a mere comparison devoid of a judgment that would make it an error inconsistent with reality—the true meaning of the verse, according to them, is that the state of these usurers is like the state of the madman confounded by Satan's touch. As for madness being attributed to the touch of Satan, they claim it is impossible because Allah, the Glorified, is too just to empower Satan over the intellect of His servant or over His believing servant.

To this it is replied: He, the Exalted, is too sublime to base His speech upon falsehood or vain talk in any manner of attribution, except while clarifying its falsehood and refuting its speaker. He, the Exalted, said in describing His speech: **لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** — "It is indeed a mighty Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it" (41:41-42); and He said: **إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ وَمَا هُوَ بِالْهَزْلِ** — "It is indeed a decisive word, and it is not a jest" (86:13-14).

As for the claim that attributing madness to the influence of Satan by removing the intellect contradicts His justice, the same objection can be turned back upon them regarding their attribution of the loss of intellect to natural causes; for these also ultimately trace back to Allah the Exalted while resulting in the loss of intellect.

Furthermore, in reality, there is no problem in the loss of intellect if Allah causes it to depart. For the religious obligation (التكليف) is lifted at that point due to the removal of its subject (الموضوع). The problem only lies in the rational perception deviating from the course of truth and the path of rectitude while the subject of the intellect remains in place—such as a rational person seeing the beautiful as ugly and vice versa, or seeing the truth as falsehood and vice versa, arbitrarily through the influence of Satan. This is what cannot be attributed to Him, the Exalted. As for the departure of the discerning power and the corruption of its judgment following the departure of the intellect itself, there is no problem in it, whether it is attributed to nature or to Satan.

Moreover, the attribution of madness to Satan is not direct and without mediation; rather, natural causes such as nerve disorders and brain afflictions are proximate causes (أسباب قريبة) behind which stands Satan—just as types of miracles (الكرامات) are attributed to the Angel despite the intervention of natural causes. Something similar has been mentioned in what Allah narrated from Job (عليه السلام) when he said: **أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الشَّيْطَانُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذَابٍ** — "Behold, Satan has touched me with hardship and pain" (38:41); and when he said: **أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الضُّرُّ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ** — "Adversity has touched me, and You are the most merciful of the merciful" (21:83). Adversity (الضر) is illness, and it has apparent natural causes in the body, yet he attributed the illness resulting from its natural causes to Satan.

This, and what resembles it, are among the materialist views that have crept into the minds of several researchers without them realizing it. When the materialists heard the divines (الإلهيين) attributing events to Allah the Glorified, or attributing some of them to the soul, the Angel, or Satan, they became confused. They thought this was a negation of natural causes and the establishment of the supernatural in their place. They did not understand that what is meant is a causation in a vertical sequence (تعليل في طول تعليل), not a horizontal one (في عرض تعليل). This has been pointed out repeatedly in previous discussions.

**Fifth:** The falsity of what some other commentators mentioned: that the purpose of the comparison is to describe the state of usurers on the Day of Resurrection, and that they will rise from their graves on the Day of Resurrection like an epileptic confounded by madness. The reason for its falsity is that the apparent meaning of the verse, as we have explained, does not support this meaning. A narration (الرواية) does not give a verse an apparent meaning that it does not possess; it merely explains the state of the usurer on the Day of Resurrection.

It is stated in *Tafsir al-Manar*: "As for the usurer rising like one whom Satan has confounded by his touch, Ibn Atiyyah said in his commentary: 'The meaning is to compare the usurer in this world to the confounded epileptic, just as it is said of one who collapses with various movements: He has gone mad.'"

**I say:** This is what first comes to mind. However, the majority (الجمهور) went against it and said: "The meaning of rising is the rising from the grave at the Resurrection, and that Allah has made it a sign of the usurers on the Day of Resurrection that they will be resurrected like epileptics." They narrated this from Ibn Abbas and Ibn Mas'ud; indeed, al-Tabarani narrated from the hadith of Awf ibn Malik in a marfu' (مرفوع) — *a narration attributed directly to the Prophet* — form: **إِياك والذنوب التي لا تغفر : الغلول فمن غل شيئاً أُتي به يوم القيامة ، والربا فمن أكل الربا بعث يوم القيامة مجنوناً يتخبط** — "Beware of the sins that are not forgiven: embezzlement (الغلول), for whoever embezzles anything shall bring it on the Day of Resurrection; and usury, for whoever devours usury shall be resurrected on the Day of Resurrection as a madman, confounded."

He then said: "What first comes to all minds is what Ibn Atiyyah said, because when 'rising' is mentioned, it refers to the habitual standing up in actions, and there is no context (قرينة) indicating that it means the Resurrection. None of these narrations are free from criticism regarding their chains of transmission (سند), and they were not revealed with the Quran, nor did the marfu' ones among them come as an explanation of the verse. Were it not for them, no one would have spoken other than what first comes to mind, which Ibn Atiyyah stated, except for one to whom its reality was not apparent."

He then said: "The fabricators (الوضاعون) who invent narrations used to target some verses of the Quran whose apparent meaning was difficult for them, and they would fabricate a narration to explain it. Rarely is anything in tafsir authentic." This is the end of what he mentioned.

He was correct in what he mentioned regarding their error, but he erred in establishing the meaning of the comparison occurring in the verse when he said: "As for what Ibn Atiyyah said, it is apparent in itself; for those whom wealth has infatuated and enslaved until their souls are consumed by its collection, making it an end in itself and abandoning for its sake all natural means of earning—their souls deviate from the moderation (الاعتدال) found in most people. This appears in their movements and their fluctuations in their deeds, as you see in the movements of those obsessed with the stock market and those addicted to gambling. Their vigor and absorption in their work increase until it becomes a lightness followed by unorganized movements. This is the aspect of similarity between their movements and the confounding of the touched person; for confounding (التخبط) is from striking (الخبط), which is unorganized striking, like the striking of a blind camel." End quote.

For what he mentioned regarding their movements deviating from moderation and order, though correct in itself, is neither purely the effect of devouring usury, nor is it the intended meaning of the comparison in the verse. As for the first, it is due to their detachment from the meaning of servitude (العبودية) and their clinging to material pleasures—"that is their reach of knowledge." Thus, they are stripped of religious chastity and psychological dignity, and their souls are affected by every minor material pleasure that appears, followed by the agitation of their movements. This is observed and felt in everyone whose state is as we described, even if they never touched usury in their entire life.

As for the second, the argument (الاحتجاج) occurring in the verse for their being confounded does not suit the aspect of similarity he mentioned. For Allah, the Glorified, argues for their being confounded in their rising by His saying: "That is because they said: 'Trade is just like usury.'" If it were as he says, it would have been more appropriate to argue for that by what he mentioned of the disorder of their movements and the corruption of order in their deeds. Thus, we must return to what we previously established.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذلك بأنهم قالوا انما البيع مثل الربا** — "That is because they said: 'Trade is just like usury.'" The reason for comparing trade to usury rather than the reverse—by saying: "Usury is just like trade"—has already been mentioned. One in whom confounding and disorder have taken root stands in a position outside of habitual rectitude; the recognized (المعروف) among the rational and the unrecognized (المنكر) are equal to him. If you command him to abandon the unrecognized act he is performing and return to the recognized, he would answer—if he answered—that what you command me to do is like what you forbid me from; it has no advantage over it. If he were to say: "What you forbid me from is like what you command me to do," he would be rational and not disordered in perception; for the meaning of this statement is that he admits that what he is commanded to do is a foundation with an advantage that must be followed, but he claims that what he is forbidden from has a similar advantage. His words would not mean the negation and disregard of the advantage, as the touched person sees it. This is the statement of the usurer in whose soul confounding has settled: "Trade is just like usury." Had he said: "Usury is just like trade," he would have been refuting Allah and denying the Sharia, not confounded like the touched person.

It appears that His saying, the Exalted: "That is because they said: 'Trade is just like usury,'" is a narration of their state (حكاية حال) which speaks thus, even if they did not say it with their tongues. This context—meaning the narration of a state through speech—is well-known among people.

By this, the falsity of what some mentioned becomes clear: that the meaning of their saying "Trade is just like usury" is to place them in the same category, and they only inverted the comparison, making usury the foundation and comparing trade to it for emphasis (المبالغة), as in the verse:

*And a desert whose regions are dust-covered,*
*As if the color of its ground were its sky.*

Similarly, the falsity of what others mentioned: that it is possible the comparison is not inverted, based on their understanding that trade was only permitted for the sake of earning and profit, and that this is realized in usury while it is illusory in others. The reason for its falsity is clear from what has preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وأحل الله البيع وحرم الربا** — "while Allah has allowed trade and forbidden usury." This is a new sentence (جملة مستأنفة), based on the rule that a verbal sentence starting with a past-tense verb, if it were a circumstantial clause (حال), would have to start with "قد". It is said: "Zayd came to me and he had struck (وقد ضرب) Amr." Moreover, its being a circumstantial clause does not suit the meaning provided by the beginning of the discourse. For the circumstantial clause is a restriction on the time of its governing verb and a vessel for its realization. If it were a circumstantial clause, it would imply that their confounding due to their saying "Trade is just like usury" only occurs while Allah has allowed trade and forbidden usury for them, whereas the matter is otherwise; they are confounded after the legislation of this permissibility and prohibition, and before their legislation. Thus, the sentence is not circumstantial but rather a new sentence.

This new sentence does not contain the initial legislation, as previously mentioned that the verses clearly indicate the prior legislation of the prohibition. Rather, it builds upon what the verse of Al-Imran indicates: "O you who have faith! Do not exact usury, twofold and severalfold, and be wary of Allah so that you may be felicitous" (3:130). Thus, the sentence—meaning His saying: "while Allah has allowed"—does not indicate the initiation of the judgment (إنشاء الحكم), but rather informs of a prior judgment and serves as a preamble to the branching of His saying after it: "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord..." This is what the apparent meaning of the noble verse leads to.

It has been said that His saying: "while Allah has allowed trade and forbidden usury" is directed toward refuting their saying: "Trade is just like usury." The meaning would be: if it were as they say, their judgment would not differ before the Wisest of Judges, yet Allah has allowed one and forbidden the other.

To this it is replied: although it is a correct argument in itself, it does not fit the wording of the verse; for it implies that the sentence "while Allah has allowed..." is circumstantial, which it is not.

Even weaker is what others mentioned: that the meaning of His saying: "while Allah has allowed..." is that the increase in the case of trade is not like the increase in the case of usury, because "I have allowed trade and forbidden usury, and the command is My command, and the creation is My creation; I judge among them as I will, and I bind them to worship as I desire; none of them has the right to object to My judgment."

To this it is replied: this is also based on taking the sentence as circumstantial rather than a new sentence. Furthermore, it is based on denying the connection of judgments to benefits (المصالح) and harms (المفاسد) as a connection of cause and effect (السببية والمسببية). In other words, it is based on the negation of causality (العلية والمعلولية) between things and attributing everything to Allah the Glorified without mediation, which necessity refutes. Moreover, it is contrary to the Quran's custom of explaining its judgments and laws by specific or general benefits. Furthermore, His saying within these verses: "and abandon what remains of usury, if you are believers," and His saying: "you shall not wrong," and His saying: "Those who devour usury..." until His saying "...like usury," all indicate a type of explanation (تعليل) for allowing trade as being in accordance with the path of nature and creation, and for prohibiting usury as being outside the paths of rectitude in life, being incompatible with faith in Allah the Exalted, and being an injustice (ظلم).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فمن جاءه موعظة من ربه فانتهى فله ما سلف وأمره الى الله** — "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists, he shall have what is past, and his matter rests with Allah." This is a branching (تفريع) from His saying: "while Allah has allowed trade..." The discourse is not restricted to usury; it is a universal judgment (حكم كلي) placed in a particular case to indicate that it is one of its instances to which its judgment applies. The meaning is: what We have mentioned to you regarding the matter of usury is an admonition that has come to you from your Lord, and whoever receives an admonition... so if you desist, you shall have what is past and your matter rests with Allah.

From this, it becomes clear: that the meaning of the "coming of the admonition" is the reaching of the judgment which Allah the Exalted has legislated; and "desisting" (الانتهاء) is repentance (التوبة) and abandoning the forbidden act out of desisting from His prohibition. From the fact that "what is past is theirs," it is understood that the judgment does not apply retrospectively to the time before it reached them. From His saying: "he shall have what is past, and his matter rests with Allah," it is understood that the eternal punishment indicated by His saying: "and whoever resumes—they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it forever," is not inevitable for them. Thus, they benefit regarding what they did in the past by escaping this destruction, and it remains that their matter rests with Allah; He may release them from some judgments, or He may impose upon them what compensates for what they missed.

Know that the matter of the verse is remarkable; for His saying: "So whoever receives an admonition..." to the end of the verse, with the ease and intensification it contains, is a judgment not specific to usury. Rather, it is general, encompassing all destructive major sins. The people have fallen short in researching its meaning by restricting themselves to the case of usury specifically—regarding the pardon for what is past, the return of the matter to Allah for one who desists, and the eternity of punishment for one who resumes after the admonition has come. All of this is despite the generality you see in the verse.

Therefore, it has become clear to you that His saying: "he shall have what is past, and his matter rests with Allah" only yields a vague meaning that becomes defined by the definition of the sin regarding which the admonition came, and it varies according to it. The meaning is: whoever desists due to an admonition that reached him, then that which preceded of the sin—whether it was regarding the rights of Allah or the rights of people—he is not taken to task for its essence. However, it does not necessarily mean he is freed from its consequences (تبعاته) as he was freed from its origin in terms of its commission. Rather, his matter therein rests with Allah: if He wills, He imposes a consequence, such as making up for missed prayers (قضاء الصلوة), broken fasts, cases of prescribed punishments (الحدود) and discretionary penalties (التعزيزات), and returning preserved wealth taken by usurpation (غصباً) or usury, and so on—while pardoning the original crimes through repentance and desisting. And if He wills, He pardons the sin and does not impose a consequence after repentance, such as a polytheist when he repents from his polytheism, or one who sinned by drinking wine or idle play between himself and Allah, and the like. For His saying: "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists" is absolute (مطلق), encompassing the disbelievers and the believers at the beginning of legislation, and others among the followers and the people of subsequent ages.

As for His saying: **ومن عاد فاولئك اصحاب النار هم فيها خالدون** — "and whoever resumes—they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it forever." The occurrence of "resuming" (العود) in this sentence in opposition to "desisting" in the previous sentence indicates that what is meant is the resumption that is coupled with a lack of desisting. This necessitates persistence (الإصرار) in the sin and non-acceptance of the judgment, which is disbelief (الكفر) or apostasy (الردة) inwardly, even if one does not utter with his tongue what indicates that. For whoever resumes a sin and does not desist from it, even through regret, has not truly submitted to the judgment and will never be successful. Thus, the choice in the verse is, in reality, between submission to the judgment—which is not devoid of the intent not to violate it—and persistence, which is usually not devoid of non-submission, necessitating eternity in the Fire, as you have learned.

From this, the answer to the Mu'tazilites' argument using the verse for the eternal punishment of the committer of a major sin becomes clear. For although the verse indicates the eternity of the committer of a major sin—indeed, anyone who commits a sin—in punishment, its indication is restricted to the commission coupled with non-submission to the judgment, and there is no problem in that.

Regarding His saying: "he shall have what is past," and His saying: "his matter rests with Allah," and His saying: "and whoever resumes...", various meanings and possibilities have been mentioned based on what the majority understood from the verse. However, we have omitted them due to their lack of benefit after the falsity of their premise.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يمحق الله الربا ويربي الصدقات** — "Allah brings usury to naught and gives increase to charities" (2:276). Annihilation (المحق) is the gradual decrease of a thing, and its falling into the path of extinction and disappearance. Increase (الإرباء) is growth (الإنماء). The "sinful" (الأثيم) is the one who carries sin (الإثم). The meaning of sin has already passed.

In the verse, a contrast is made between the increase of charities and the annihilation of usury. It has already been mentioned that the increase and growth of charities is not restricted to the Hereafter; rather, it is a characteristic that is general, encompassing this world as well as the Hereafter. Thus, the annihilation of usury is inevitably the same.

Just as it is a characteristic of charities that they grow wealth—a growth that follows them necessarily and is inseparable from them, in that they spread mercy, bequeath love and good understanding, harmonize hearts, and spread security and preservation, turning hearts away from intending anger, misappropriation, corruption, and theft, and calling toward unity, assistance, and cooperation, thereby closing most paths of corruption and extinction that afflict wealth, all of which aids in the growth of wealth and its yielding manifold increases—

Similarly, it is a characteristic of usury that it annihilates wealth and destroys it gradually, in that it spreads cruelty and loss, bequeaths hatred, enmity, and ill-suspicion, corrupts security and preservation, and incites souls toward revenge by any possible means—whether by word or deed, directly or indirectly—and calls toward division and disagreement, thereby opening most paths of corruption and doors of disappearance upon wealth. Rarely does wealth escape an affliction that strikes it or a calamity that encompasses it.

All of this is because these two matters—charity and usury—are connected to and touch upon the lives of the class of the poor and the needy. Their inner feelings have been stirred by necessary need, and their oppressed, humiliated souls have become ready to defend the rights of life, intending to retaliate to the utmost. If they are treated with kindness through good deeds and favors without compensation—in such a state—their feelings turn toward reciprocating with kindness and good intention, producing a beautiful effect. But if they are treated poorly through acts of cruelty, harshness, and the taking of wealth, honor, and life, they reciprocate with revenge and harm by any means. Rarely does anyone among the usurers escape the consequences of these destructive intentions, as everyone mentions from what they have witnessed of the news of usurers regarding the loss of their wealth, the ruin of their homes, and the failure of their efforts.

You must know, **first:** that the causes and reasons (العلعل والاسباب) upon which social matters and events are built are matters of predominant existence and influence (أغلبية الوجود والتأثير). For we only intend by our actions their goals and results whose realization is predominant, and we create, when intending them, their causes from which their effects are not separated in most cases, though not always. We treat the rare exception as non-existent. As for the complete causes (العلل التامة) from which their effects cannot possibly be separated in existence, they are specific to the realm of creation (التكوين) and are dealt with by the real sciences (العلوم الحقيقية) that research external realities.

Contemplation of the verses of judgments (آيات الاحكام) in which the benefits and harms of deeds and actions are mentioned—leading to felicity or wretchedness—shows that the Quran, in building the effects of deeds upon the deeds and building the deeds upon their causes, follows this path and places the predominant in the position of the constant, as is the practice of the rational.

**Second:** That society is like the individual, and the social matter is like the individual matter; they are similar in states according to what suits each in terms of existence. Just as the individual has a life, a lifespan, a fixed death, and actions and effects, so too does society have its life, death, lifespan, actions, and effects. The Quran speaks of this, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا وَلَهَا كِتَابٌ مَّعْلُومٌ مَّا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ** — "We have not destroyed any town but that it had a known term. No community can advance its term, nor can they defer it" (15:4-5).

Accordingly, if the description of a matter changes from individual to social, the manner of its persistence, disappearance, and effect changes. Individual chastity and individual profligacy, while they are individual, have a type of influence on life; for committing indecency, for example, causes people to be repelled by the person, to avoid marrying him or sitting with him, and leads to the loss of trust in his honesty—this is when it is an individual matter and society is contrary to it. But when it becomes social and well-known among the public, those deterrents disappear because they were consequences of public disapproval and general loathing of the act, which circulation and prevalence have removed. However, the natural corruptions—such as the cessation of lineage, venereal diseases, and other social corruptions that nature does not accept, like the nullification and disorder of lineages and the corruption of communal branches and the social benefits resulting from them—are inevitably resulting from it. Similarly, the appearance of effects in the individual when it was individual differs from their appearance in society when it is social in terms of speed and slowness.

Once you know this, you will know that His annihilation of usury in contrast to His increasing of charities inevitably differs between when the act is an individual act—like usury practiced by a person, for it usually destroys its practitioner, and rarely does a usurer escape it due to the presence of specific causes and factors that ward off extinction and humiliation from the arena of his life—and when it is a social act, like the usury circulating today which nations and states recognize officially, for which laws have been established, and upon which banks have been founded. For it loses some of its individual characteristics due to the society's acceptance of what has become prevalent and customary among them, and the turning of souls away from thinking about its defects. However, its necessary effects—such as the accumulation and concentration of public wealth on one side, and the occurrence of public poverty and deprivation on the other, and the appearance of complete separation and gap between the two groups: the wealthy and the destitute—are inseparable from this usury and will surely produce their evil, ill-omened effect. This type of appearance and manifestation, although we may perceive it as slow from an individual perspective and perhaps disregard it by treating it as non-existent due to the length of time, is immediate from a social perspective; for the social lifespan is not like the individual lifespan, and a social day may equal an age in the view of an individual. The Exalted said: **وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ** — "We make such days alternate among mankind" (3:140). This "day" refers to the era in which some people prevail over others, one group over another, one government over another, and one nation over another. It is clear that just as the felicity of man must be cared for in terms of the individual, it must be cared for in terms of the social species.

The Quran does not speak only of the individual nor only in the individual, though it is not silent about him. Rather, it is a Book revealed by Allah the Exalted as a guardian over the felicity of man: his species and his individual, and as a witness over the felicity of the world: its present and its past.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: "Allah brings usury to naught and gives increase to charities" explains the state of usury and charity in their effects, whether they are social or individual. Annihilation is among the necessary consequences of usury, inseparable from it, just as increase is among the necessary consequences of charity, inseparable from it. Thus, usury is annihilated even if it is called "increase" (رباً), and charity is a truly increasing usury even if it is not called usury. To this He, the Exalted, points: "Allah brings usury to naught and gives increase to charities" by giving the description of usury to charities of all kinds, and describing usury with a description that contradicts its name in terms of meaning, which is annihilation.

From the preceding explanation, the weakness of what some mentioned becomes clear: that the annihilation of usury does not mean the nullification of effort and the loss of work through the disappearance of usurious wealth, for observation and sight refute this; rather, what is meant by annihilation is the nullification of effort in terms of the goals intended by this type of transaction. For the usurer intends by collecting wealth in this way the pleasure of ease, the goodness of life, and the enjoyment of living, but he is distracted from that by the obsession with collecting wealth and placing dirham upon dirham, and the struggle against those who intend evil for him, his wealth, or his profits, and the anxieties attacking his soul from the enmity of people and the hatred of the needy for him. The reason for its weakness is clear.

Similarly, what others mentioned: that what is meant by it is the annihilation of the Hereafter and the reward of deeds from which the usurer turns away by his preoccupation with usury, or which the use of usurious wealth nullifies, such as types of worship. The reason for its weakness is that there is no doubt that what they mentioned is part of the annihilation, but there is no evidence for its restriction to that.

Similarly, the weakness of what the Mu'tazilites used as an argument for the eternal punishment of the committer of a major sin in the Fire by His saying: "and whoever resumes...", and what has passed clarifies both the establishment of the argument and its refutation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِن الله لا يحب كل كفار أثيم** — "Allah does not like any sinful ingrate" (2:276). This is an explanation (تعليل) for the annihilation of usury in a universal way. The meaning is that the devourer of usury is a great ingrate (كفار) due to his ingratitude for many of Allah's blessings by his veiling the natural paths in human life—which are the paths of natural transactions—and his disbelief in many judgments regarding the prescribed acts of worship and transactions. For by spending usurious wealth on his food, drink, clothing, and dwelling, he nullifies many of his acts of worship due to the loss of conditions required in them. By using the usurious wealth in his hand, he nullifies many of his transactions, becomes liable for others, and usurps the wealth of others in many instances. By employing greed and avarice regarding people's wealth, and harshness and cruelty in exacting what he considers his right, he corrupts many of the foundations of ethics and virtues and their branches. He is "sinful" (أثيم)—one in whose soul sin has settled. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, does not love him because Allah does not love any sinful ingrate.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِن الذين آمنوا وعملوا الصالحات** — "Indeed, those who have faith and do righteous deeds..." (2:277). This is an explanation by which the reward of the charitable and those who desist from what Allah has forbidden of devouring usury is clarified in a general way that applies to the case perfectly.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أيها الذين آمنوا اتقوا الله وذروا ما بقي من الربا ان كنتم مؤمنين** — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and abandon what remains of usury, if you are believers" (2:278). This is an address to the believers and a command to them to be wary of Allah, which is a preamble to what follows of the command: "and abandon what remains of usury." It indicates that among the believers at the time of the revelation of the verses were those who took usury and had remnants of it owed by people; thus, they were commanded to abandon them, and were threatened regarding that by what follows of His saying: "But if you do not, then be informed of a war from Allah and His Apostle."

This supports what we will narrate of the narration regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verse in the following tradition-based discussion.

In restricting the discourse with His saying: "if you are believers," there is an indication that abandoning it is among the requirements of faith, and a confirmation of what preceded of His saying: "and whoever resumes...", and His saying: "Allah does not like any sinful ingrate."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وإِن لم تفعلوا فأذنوا بحرب من الله ورسوله** — "But if you do not, then be informed of a war from Allah and His Apostle" (2:279). The word "اذن" (be informed) is like "علم" (know) in weight and meaning. It has been recited as "فآذنوا" as a command from "ايذان" (to inform). The "ب" in His saying "بحرب" (of a war) is to imply the meaning of certainty and the like. The meaning is: be certain of a war, or inform yourselves with certainty of a war from Allah and His Apostle. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "war" is to indicate its greatness or its variety. The attribution of the war to Allah and His Apostle is because it is connected to the judgment in which Allah the Glorified has a share through establishment and legislation, and His Apostle has a share through transmission. If it were from Allah alone, it would be a creative (تكويني) matter; as for His Apostle, he does not act independently in any matter apart from Allah the Glorified. The Exalted said: **لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ** — "You have no hand in the matter" (3:128).

War from Allah and His Apostle regarding one of the judgments against one who does not submit to it is the enforcement of the judgment upon the one who rejects it among the Muslims through fighting, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **فَقَاتِلُوا الَّتِي تَبْغِي حَتَّىٰ تَفِيءَ إِلَىٰ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ** — "then fight the one that is rebellious until it reverts to Allah’s command" (49:9). Furthermore, Allah the Exalted has another way of defending His judgment, which is His warring against them through the path of nature—by inciting the general nature against them, which cuts off their breaths, destroys their homes, and erases their traces. The Exalted said: **وَإِذَا أَرَدْنَا أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا فِيهَا فَحقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَاهَا تَدْمِيرًا** — "When We desire to destroy a town, We command its affluent ones [to obey Us]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly" (17:16).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وإِن تبتم فلكم رؤس أموالكم لا تظلمون ولا تظلمون** — "And if you repent, then you shall have your principal, neither tyrannizing nor being tyrannized" (2:279). The word "And if you repent" supports what has passed—that the address in the verse is to some of the believers who were taking usury and had remnants owed by their debtors and clients. His saying: "then you shall have your principal" (رؤس أموالكم) means the original amounts of your wealth free from usury. "Neither tyrannizing" by taking usury, "nor being tyrannized" by encroachment upon your principal. In the verse, there is an indication of the confirmation of the origin of ownership (الملك) **first**; and that taking usury is an injustice (ظلم) as previously mentioned **second**; and the confirmation of types of transactions where He expressed it as "your principal" (رؤس أموالكم), for wealth is only called "principal" (رأس مال) when it is spent in the ways of transactions and types of earning **third**.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وإِن كان ذو عسرة فنظرة إِلى ميسرة** — "And if [the debtor] is in financial difficulty, let there be a respite until a time of ease" (2:280). The word "كان" is complete (تامة), meaning: if there exists one in difficulty. "النظرة" is the respite (المهلة). "الميسرة" is ease (اليسار) and ability, the opposite of difficulty. That is: if a debtor (غريم) among your debtors is found who is unable to pay his due debt, then give him respite and delay him until he is able and possesses ease, then he may pay his debt.

The verse, although absolute and not restricted, applies to the case of usury. For they used to, when the term of the debt arrived, demand it from the debtor. The debtor would say to his creditor: "Increase my term by such a duration, and I will increase the price for you by such a ratio." The verse forbids this usurious increase and commands respite.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وان تصدقوا خير لكم إِن كنتم تعلمون** — "And that you should give it as charity is better for you, if you knew" (2:280). That is: if you remit the debt for the one in difficulty and give it as charity to him, it is better for you if you knew; for you have then replaced what you intended of increase through the path of annihilated usury with the increase through the path of truly increasing charity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **واتقوا يوما ترجعون فيه إِلى الله** — "And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah" (2:281). In this is a concluding remark (تذييل) for the verses of usury, including the judgment and the recompense, by a general reminder of the Day of Resurrection with some of its descriptions suited to the context. Its mention prepares the souls for the godwariness (تقوى) of Allah and the refraining from His prohibitions regarding the rights of people upon which life relies. It is that before you is a day in which you will be brought back to Allah, then every soul will be paid in full what it has earned, and they will not be wronged.

As for the meaning of this "return" despite our not being absent from Allah, and the meaning of this "paying in full," the discussion on it will come in the commentary on Surah Al-An'am, Allah the Exalted willing.

It has been said that this verse: "And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah, then every soul will be paid in full what it has earned, and they will not be wronged," is the last verse revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). What indicates this from the narrations will come in the following tradition-based discussion.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Those who devour usury...", narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When I was taken on the Night Journey (أُسري بي) to the heavens, I saw a people, one of whom would want to rise but could not rise due to the greatness of his belly. I said: Who are these, O Gabriel? He said: These are those who devour usury; they shall not rise except like one whom Satan has confounded by his touch. And behold, they are on the path of the people of Pharaoh: they are exposed to the Fire morning and evening, and they say: Our Lord, when will the Hour arrive?'"

**I say:** This is a barzakh-related (برزخي) — *pertaining to the intermediate realm between death and resurrection* — example and a confirmation of his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "As you live, you shall die; and as you die, you shall be resurrected."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Asbahani narrated in his *Targhib* from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The devourer of usury will come on the Day of Resurrection as a madman (مختبلاً) dragging his sides.' Then he recited: 'they shall not rise except like one whom Satan has confounded by his touch.'"

**I say:** Many narrations have been reported regarding the punishment for usury through the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis. In some of them, it is stated that it is equivalent to seventy acts of adultery committed by the usurer with his mother.

In *al-Tahdhib*, through his chain of narrators from 'Umar ibn Yazid, the seller of Sabiri-cloth, who said: "I said to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'May I be your ransom! People claim that profit (الربح) made from a person in distress (المضطر) is forbidden (حرام).'" He replied: "Have you ever seen anyone, whether rich or poor, buy anything except out of necessity (ضرورة)? O 'Umar, Allah has made trade lawful and forbidden usury; so take a profit, but do not practice usury." I asked: "And what is usury (الربا)?" He said: "Dirhams for dirhams, two for one; and wheat for wheat, two for one."

In *al-Faqih*, through his chain of narrators from 'Ubayd ibn Zurarah, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Usury (الربا) only occurs in that which is measured by volume (يكال) or weighed (يوزن)."

**I say:** There has been disagreement regarding the items in which usury occurs, resulting in various opinions. The doctrine of the People of the House (peace be upon them) is that it only occurs in the two currencies [gold and silver] and in what is measured or weighed. This is a matter of jurisprudence (فقهية), and our purpose here only concerns it to this extent.

In *al-Kafi*, from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq], and in the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: **فَمَن جَاءَهُ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِ فَانتَهَى** — "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists" (2:275), he said: "The admonition (الموعظة) is repentance (التوبة)."

In *al-Tahdhib*, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "A man from the people of Khurasan came to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him). He had dealt in usury until his wealth became vast. He then asked the jurists (الفقهاء), and they said: 'Nothing will be accepted from you until you return it to its owners.' He then came to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and related his story to him. Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: 'Your way out is from the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَمَن جَاءَهُ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِ فَانتَهَى فَلَهُ مَا سَلَفَ وَأَمْرُهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ** — "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists, he shall keep what is past, and his case will rest with Allah." He said: 'The admonition is repentance.'"

In *al-Kafi* and *al-Faqih*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Every instance of usury that people consumed out of ignorance (جهالة) and then repented, it shall be accepted from them once their repentance (التوبة) is known." He also said: "If a man inherits wealth from his father and knows that there is usury in that wealth, but it has been mixed with other funds in trade, then it is lawful (حلال) for him and he may consume it. But if he recognizes a specific, known portion of it, he should take his principal (رأس ماله) and return the excess (الزيادة)."

In *al-Faqih* and *al-'Uyun*, from al-Rida (peace be upon him): "It is a major sin (كبيرة) after the clarification (البيان)." He said: "And to treat that with light regard is an entry into disbelief (الكفر)."

In *al-Kafi*: He was asked about a man who consumes usury while believing it to be lawful. He said: "It does not harm him until he does it intentionally (متعمداً). Once he does it intentionally, he is in the position described by Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

In *al-Kafi* and *al-Faqih*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), having been asked about the saying of the Exalted: **يَمْحَقُ اللَّهُ الرِّبَا وَيُرْبِي الصَّدَقَاتِ** — "Allah brings usury to naught and He makes charities flourish" (2:276). It was said to him: "I see one who consumes usury and his wealth increases (يربو)?" He replied: "What 'bringing to naught' (محق) is more destructive than a dirham of usury? It brings the religion (الدين) to naught, and even if he repents, his wealth departs and he becomes impoverished."

**I say:** The narration, as you see, interprets the legislative nullification (المحق التشريعي) — meaning the lack of recognition of ownership and the prohibition — and charity stands in contrast to it in this regard. This does not contradict what was previously mentioned regarding the universality of this nullification.

In *al-Majma'*, from 'Ali (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) cursed five types of people regarding usury: the one who consumes it, the one who pays it, its two witnesses, and its scribe."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through various chains from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم).

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah the Exalted said: 'I am the Creator of everything, and I have entrusted things to others besides Me, except for charity (الصدقة); for I receive it with My own hand. Even if a man or woman gives half a date in charity, I nurture it (أربيها) for them just as one of you nurtures his weaned foal (فصيله) or young colt (فلوه), until I return it to him on the Day of Resurrection greater than Mount Uhud.'"

In the same source, from 'Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon them), from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), who said: "Indeed, Allah nurtures charity for one of you just as one of you nurtures his child, until he meets Him on the Day of Resurrection and it is like Uhud."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated through Sunni chains from several companions, such as Abu Hurayrah, 'Aishah, Ibn 'Umar, and Abu Barzah al-Aslami, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: When Allah revealed: **الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ الرِّبَا** — "Those who exact usury..." (2:275), Khalid ibn al-Walid stood before the Messenger of Allah and said: "O Messenger of Allah, my father's usury is with the tribe of Thaqif, and he charged me at his death to collect it." Then Allah revealed: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَذَرُوا مَا بَقِيَ مِنَ الرِّبَا** — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and abandon [your claims to] what remains of usury..." (2:278).

**I say:** A similar account is narrated in *al-Majma'* from al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

In *al-Majma'* also, from al-Suddi and 'Ikrimah, they said: "It was revealed concerning a remainder of usury belonging to al-'Abbas and Khalid ibn al-Walid. They were partners during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), lending with usury to the Banu 'Amr ibn 'Umayr, a group from Thaqif. When Islam came, they had vast amounts of wealth in usury. So Allah revealed this verse, and the Prophet said: 'Behold! Every instance of usury from the Age of Ignorance is abolished (موضوع), and the first usury I abolish is the usury of al-'Abbas ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib. And every blood-feud from the Age of Ignorance is abolished, and the first blood-feud I abolish is the blood of Rabi'ah ibn al-Harith ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib, who was being nursed among the Banu Layth and was killed by Hudhayl.'"

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim, from al-Suddi, except that it says: "It was revealed concerning al-'Abbas ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib and a man from the Banu al-Mughirah."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* narrated from 'Amr ibn al-Ahwas: "That he attended the Farewell Pilgrimage with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), who said: 'Behold! Every instance of usury from the Age of Ignorance is abolished. You shall have your principal wealth (رؤوس أموالكم); you shall neither wrong nor be wronged.'"

**I say:** The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous. What is gathered from the narrations of both the elite (الخاصة) [Shi'a] and the general (العامة) [Sunni] traditions is that the verse was revealed concerning usurious wealth owed to the Banu al-Mughirah by the Thaqif. They used to practice usury with them during the Age of Ignorance. When Islam came, they demanded the remainders owed to them, but the others refused to pay because Islam had abolished it. The matter was raised to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and the verse was revealed.

This supports what we previously stated in the Commentary: that usury was forbidden in Islam before the revelation of these verses and had been clarified to the people, and that these verses only emphasize and affirm the prohibition. Therefore, no weight is given to some reports suggesting that the prohibition of usury was only revealed at the end of the Messenger of Allah's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) life and that he passed away without clarifying the matter of usury to the people—as found in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Jarir and Ibn Mardawayh from 'Umar ibn al-Khattab: "That he gave a sermon and said: 'Among the last of the Qur'an to be revealed was the verse of usury, and the Messenger of Allah died without clarifying it to us; so leave what makes you doubt for what does not make you doubt.'"

Furthermore, it is the doctrine of the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them) that Allah the Exalted did not take His Prophet until He had legislated everything the people needed regarding their religion, and His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) had clarified that to the people.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, through numerous chains from Ibn 'Abbas, al-Suddi, 'Atiyyah al-'Awfi, Abu Salih, and Sa'id ibn Jubayr: "The last verse revealed of the Qur'an was the saying of the Exalted: **وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى اللَّهِ** — 'And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah...'" (2:281).

In *al-Majma'*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The prohibition of usury was made so severe only so that people would not refrain from performing acts of kindness (اصطناع المعروف) through lending (قرضاً) or assistance (رفداً)."

In *al-Majma'* also, from 'Ali (peace be upon him): "When Allah intends the destruction of a town, usury becomes manifest among them."

**I say:** The meaning of these narrations was clarified in the preceding Commentary.

In the same source, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِن كَانَ ذُو عُسْرَةٍ فَنَظِرَةٌ إِلَى مَيْسَرَةٍ** — "And if [the debtor] is in financial difficulty, let there be a respite until a time of ease" (2:280), he said: "There is disagreement regarding the limit of insolvency (الإعسار). It is narrated from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: 'It is when he is unable to provide what exceeds his own sustenance and the sustenance of his family with moderation (الاقتصاد).'"

In the same source: "Granting respite to the insolvent (المعسر) is obligatory (واجب) for every debt." This is from Ibn 'Abbas, al-Dahhak, and al-Hasan, and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them).

In the same source: Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "**إِلَى مَيْسَرَةٍ** (until a time of ease) means when news of him reaches the Imam, who then pays on his behalf from the share of the debtors (سهم الغارمين), provided he spent it on what is right (المعروف)."

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah ascended the pulpit one day, praised and glorified Allah, invoked blessings upon His prophets, and then said: 'O people! Let those present among you inform those who are absent. Behold! Whoever grants respite to an insolvent person, he shall have from Allah every day a charity (صدقة) equal to his wealth until he recovers it.' Then Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: '**وَإِن كَانَ ذُو عُسْرَةٍ فَنَظِرَةٌ إِلَى مَيْسَرَةٍ وَأَن تَصَدَّقُوا خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** — "And if [the debtor] is in financial difficulty, let there be a respite until a time of ease. And that you remit it as charity is better for you, should you know" (2:280)—if you know he is insolvent, then remit your wealth to him as charity, for that is better for you.'"

**I say:** The narration contains an interpretation of His saying: **إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** (should you know). Another meaning for it has already been mentioned. The narrations regarding these meanings and what pertains to them are numerous, and the reference for them is the "Book of Debt" (كتاب الدين) in jurisprudence.

## ( A Scientific Discussion )

It has been mentioned repeatedly in previous discussions that man has no concern in his life except to perform those actions that acquire for him his existential perfections (كمالاته الوجودية); in other words, to fulfill his material needs. Thus, he performs an action related to matter in some way, through which he fulfills a vital need. He is the owner (مالك) of his labor and what he has produced—labor (العمل) in this context is broader than active doing or passive undergoing; it is a relation (نسبة) and a link (رابطة) upon which social effects are based among people. That is, he reserves the material he has worked upon for himself and considers it property (ملكاً) in which he is permitted to dispose as an individual. The rational people (العقلاء) of society permit him this; so understand.

However, since he cannot fulfill all his needs through his own labor alone, this led to cooperative society (الاجتماع التعاوني) and to each person benefiting from the labor of others and what others have acquired and owned through their labor. This led to exchange (المعاوضة) between them. This became established by man working in one field or a limited number of fields of labor, thereby owning things, then taking the amount that fulfills his need and exchanging the surplus for what he lacks from the property of others. This is the origin of transaction (المعاملة) and exchange.

However, the total disparity between goods and commodities in terms of type, the intensity or weakness of the need for them, and their abundance or scarcity, created difficulties in exchange. For instance, fruit is for eating, a donkey for carrying, water for drinking, and a precious gem for adornment. These have different weights and values in the needs of life, and different ratios to one another.

Thus, the need arose to establish value (القيمة) by instituting coins (الفلوس), dirhams, and dinars. The basis for their institution was that they took something from the commodities that were rare, such as gold, and made it a standard (أصل) to which all other commodities and goods were referred. It became like a complete unit of a species used as a measure for the rest of its members, like weights (المثاقيل) and measures (المكائيل). Thus, a single unit of currency was used to estimate general value, and every commodity was priced by it. Through this, the ratio of each commodity to the currency, and the ratio of commodities to one another, was determined.

Then, to generalize the benefit, they established units of measurement for things, such as the unit of length like the cubit (الذراع), the unit of volume like the *kayl* (الكيل), and the unit of weight like the *mann* (المن). At that point, ratios were determined, ambiguity was removed, and it became clear, for example, that a carat of diamond equals four dinars, and a *mann* of wheat flour equals one-tenth of a dinar. Thus, it became clear that a carat of diamond equals forty *manns* of wheat flour, and so on.

They then expanded by instituting other currencies from various materials, whether precious or cheap, for ease and expansion, such as silver, copper, bronze, and paper money (الورق والنوط), as explained in books of economics.

Subsequently, the door of earning (الكسب) and trade (التجارة) was opened after buying and selling became widespread, by certain individuals specializing their labor and occupation in exchange—exchanging one type of commodity for another seeking profit (الربح), which is a type of increase in what one receives in exchange for the commodity one gives.

These are actions man has put forward to fulfill his needs in life. Eventually, the matter settled such that public need seemed to revolve around the door of the dirham and dinar. The medium of value became as if it were the entirety of wealth, and as if it were every commodity man needs, because by obtaining it, man is able to obtain everything he desires and needs for enjoyment in life. Sometimes it was treated as a commodity itself and traded for profit, as one trades in other goods; this is money-changing (الصرف).

From what has preceded, it is clear that the origin of transaction and exchange was established on the basis of exchanging one commodity for another different (مغاير) from it, due to the pressing need for the substitute, as in the origin of exchange; or due to the need for profit, which is an increase in the value of the substitute. This—meaning disparity (المغايرة)—is the principle upon which the life of society relies. As for a transaction involving the exchange of a commodity for its like in kind or its equivalent: if it is without increase, such as a loan (قرض) of like for like, the rational people may recognize it due to the need for it; it is something that sustains society and fulfills the need of the destitute, and no corruption results from it. But if it is with an increase in the substitute, which is profit, then that is usury (الربا). So let us look at the result of usury.

Usury—by which we mean the exchange of like for like with an increase, such as giving ten for a term, or giving a commodity worth ten for a term and taking twelve when the term expires, and the like—only occurs when the buyer or borrower is compelled (اضطرار) to take it due to insolvency (الإعسار) and want (الإعواز), such that the extent of his need exceeds the amount of wealth he earns. For example, he earns ten for his day in an average state, but he needs twenty; so he borrows the remaining ten for twelve to be paid tomorrow. This necessitates that tomorrow he will have eight while needing twenty. From there, the average of his livelihood and life begins to vanish (الانمحاق) and diminish. It will not be long before the entirety of what he earns is exhausted and only the entirety of what he borrows remains. He is then demanded to pay twenty while he has nothing (20 - 0 = Wealth). This is destruction and the annihilation of one's effort in life.

As for the usurer (المرابي), the ten that was his own and the ten that belonged to the borrower accumulate with him, totaling twenty. Thus, both sums of wealth accumulate on one side, while the other side is emptied of wealth. This is solely because the increase was taken without any financial counter-value (عوض مالي). Thus, usury leads to the annihilation of the class of the insolvent and the dragging of wealth toward the class of the wealthy. This leads to the rule of the affluent usurers and their domination over people's wealth, honor, and lives in pursuit of all they desire and crave, due to the human instinct for superiority (التعالي) and exploitation (الاستخدام). It also leads to those exploited and humiliated defending themselves against the bitterness of life they have fallen into by every means of defense and revenge they can muster. This is chaos (الهرج والمرج) and the corruption of the system (فساد النظام), in which lies the destruction of humanity and the annihilation of civilization.

This is in addition to what frequently happens regarding the total loss of usurious wealth; for not every debtor upon whom debts have accumulated is able or willing to pay his debts.

This is regarding usury practiced between the rich and the people of hardship. As for that which occurs between others, such as commercial usury (الربا التجاري) upon which the affairs of banks and others are conducted—like usury on loans for trade—the least of its harms is that it causes wealth to be gradually dragged toward the capital set aside for usury on one hand, and causes the increase of commercial capitals and their power beyond what they actually are in reality. This leads to aggression between them, where they consume one another, some are absorbed into others, and everything is annihilated into that which is stronger than it. Thus, it continues to increase the number of those in need through insolvency, and wealth accumulates by being restricted to the few. The same peril we mentioned earlier returns.

No researcher in the fields of economics doubts that the sole reason for the spread of Communism (الشيوعية) and the advancement of the Socialist (الاشتراك) aim is the obscene accumulation of wealth among individuals and their prominent advancement in the advantages of life, while others—who are the majority—are deprived of their most essential necessities. The powerful class had deceived these weak ones with the words they struck their ears with, such as "civilization," "justice," "freedom," and "equality in human rights." They were saying with their mouths what was not in their hearts, intending by them meanings that are, in reality, the opposites of their meanings. They thought these would aid them in their desired luxury, the humiliation of the lower class, superiority over them, and absolute control as they pleased. They thought it was the only means for their happiness in life, but it was not long before what they thought was for them turned against them. Their plot and deception returned upon themselves; "they plotted, and Allah plotted, and Allah is the best of plotters." Allah the Glorified knows best what this human state will become in its future days. Among the ominous corruptions of usury is its facilitating the way to the hoarding (كنز) of wealth, and the imprisonment of thousands and millions in bank vaults away from circulation in buying and selling, and the sitting of a group on the throne of idleness and luxury, while others are deprived of the project to which the primordial nature (الفطرة) guides—which is man's reliance in his life upon labor (العمل). Thus, some do not live by labor due to their luxury, and others do not live by it due to their deprivation.

## ( Another Scientific Discussion )

Al-Ghazali said in the "Book of Gratitude" from *al-Ihya'*: "Among the blessings of Allah the Exalted is the creation of dirhams and dinars, by which the world is sustained.
