They are two stones with no utility in their physical essences (أعيانهما), but creation is compelled to use them because every human is in need of many specific items for his food, clothing, and other needs. He may lack what he needs while possessing what he can do without, such as one who possesses saffron but needs a camel to ride, while the one who possesses the camel may not need it but needs saffron. Thus, there must be an exchange between them, and there must be an estimation of the amount of the substitute. For the owner of the camel will not give his camel for just any amount of saffron, and there is no inherent relationship (مناسبة) between saffron and a camel such that it could be said: 'Give its equivalent in weight or form.' Likewise, for one who buys a house with clothes, or a slave with a shoe, or flour with a donkey—there is no inherent relationship between these things. Thus, it is not known how much the camel is worth in saffron, making transactions extremely difficult. Therefore, these disparate and distant items required a mediator (متوسط) between them to judge with a just judgment, so that the rank and status of each could be known. Once the ranks are established and the levels ordered, the equal can be known from the unequal. So Allah the Exalted created dinars and dirhams as two judges and mediators between properties so that properties could be estimated by them. It is said: 'This camel is worth a hundred dinars, and this amount of saffron is worth a hundred.' Thus, insofar as they are equal to one thing, they are equal to each other. Balancing was only possible through the two currencies because there is no purpose in their physical essences. If there were a purpose in their essences, that specific purpose might necessitate a preference for the one who has that purpose, but not for the one who does not, and the system would not be orderly. Therefore, Allah the Exalted created them to be circulated by hands and to be just judges between properties.

And for another wisdom: which is using them as a means to all other things, because they are precious (عزيزان) in themselves, yet there is no purpose in their essences. Their relationship to all other properties is the same. So whoever owns them, it is as if he owns everything—unlike one who owns a garment, for he only owns the garment. If he needs food, the owner of the food might not desire the garment because his purpose is, for example, an animal. Thus, there was a need for something else which, in its form, is as if it were nothing, but in its meaning, is as if it were all things. A thing's relationship to different things is only equal when it has no specific form of its own that provides a specific benefit, like a mirror which has no color but reflects every color. Likewise, currency has no purpose in itself but is a means to every purpose. It is like a particle (الحرف) which has no meaning in itself but through which meanings appear in others. This is the second wisdom. And in them are also other wisdoms whose mention would be lengthy."

He then said, in summary: "Since they were from Allah the Exalted for the sake of these wisdoms based upon them, whoever uses them in a way that contradicts the intended wisdoms has been ungrateful (كفر) for the blessing of Allah."

He derived from this the prohibition of hoarding (كنز) them, for it is an injustice (ظلم) and an invalidation of their wisdom. Hoarding them is like imprisoning the judge of the people in a jail, preventing him from judging between the people and casting chaos among them without the presence of one to whom they can return for justice.

He also derived from it the prohibition of using gold and silver vessels, for in that is the intention of them for their own sake (بالاستقلال), whereas they are intended for other than themselves. That is an injustice, like one who employs the judge of the city in weaving, tax-collecting, and the works performed by the lowest of people.

He also derived from it the prohibition of usurious transactions (معاملة الربا) with dirhams and dinars, for it is ingratitude for the blessing and an injustice. For they were created for other than themselves, not for themselves, as no purpose pertains to their physical essences.

The matter was confused for him regarding the consideration of their origin and the branches he derived from it:

**Firstly:** He mentioned that no purpose pertains to them in themselves. If that were so, it would not be possible for them to estimate other commodities and needs. How can one thing estimate another by something it does not possess? Can a cubit measure the length of something except by the length it possesses? Or can a *mann* estimate the weight of something except by the weight within it?

Furthermore, his admission that they are "precious in themselves" is inconsistent except with their being intended for themselves. How can "preciousness" and "honor" be conceived without being desired?

Moreover, if they were only intended for other than themselves by creation, there would be no difference in consideration between the dinar and the dirham—meaning gold and silver—yet reality belies this. All types of currency would have equal values, and consideration would not have fallen upon other commodities like leather, salt, and others.

**Secondly:** Because the wisdom necessitating the prohibition of hoarding is not the granting of independent purpose to them. Rather, what appears from the saying of the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَالْفِضَّةَ وَلَا يُنفِقُونَهَا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ** — "As for those who treasure up gold and silver and do not spend them in the way of Allah..." (9:34), is the deprivation of the poor from sustaining themselves through them, while the need for labor and exchange is constant, as the explanation of this will come in the commentary of that verse.

**Thirdly:** Because the reason he mentioned for the prohibition of using gold and silver vessels—it being an injustice and ingratitude—is present in the use of jewelry made from them, as well as in money-changing (بيع الصرف), yet these are not considered in the Law (الشرع) as injustice, ingratitude, or forbidden.

**Fourthly:** Because the corruption he mentioned, if it were the cause of the injustice and ingratitude he described, would apply to money-changing in general, just as it applies to usurious transactions involving credit (النسيئة) and loans. Yet it would not apply to usury in measured and weighed goods, even though the ruling is the same. Thus, what he mentioned is incomplete, neither inclusive nor exclusive.

What the Exalted mentioned regarding the wisdom of the prohibition corresponds to what we have put forward: taking an increase without a counter-value. The Exalted said: **وَمَا آتَيْتُم مِّن رِّبًا لِّيَرْبُوَ فِي أَمْوَالِ النَّاسِ فَلَا يَرْبُو عِندَ اللَّهِ وَمَا آتَيْتُم مِّن زَكَاةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللَّهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ** — "What you give by way of usury, so that it may increase in people’s wealth, does not increase with Allah. But what you give by way of zakat, seeking Allah’s pleasure—it is they who will receive a manifold increase" (30:39). He made usury something that "increases" (رابياً) in people's wealth; that is because it grows by attaching parts of people's wealth to itself, just as the seed of a plant grows by feeding from the earth and attaching its parts to itself. Thus, usury continues to grow and increase itself while diminishing people's wealth until it reaches the end of it. This is what we mentioned previously. By this, it becomes clear that the meaning of the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَلَكُمْ رُؤُوسُ أَمْوَالِكُمْ لَا تَظْلِمُونَ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ** — "And if you repent, then you shall have your principal, neither wronging nor being wronged" (2:279), means you shall not wrong the people, nor shall you be wronged by them or by Allah the Glorified. Thus, usury is an injustice (ظلم) against the people.
